modules.title: Python Foundations
modules.description: AST parsing, subprocess, and typed Python for AI tooling

sections.title: What to learn
sections.implementation: Build an AST parser that extracts: 1) All class definitions with their methods, 2) All function definitions, 3) All function calls. Use the ast module to walk through Python source code and collect metadata about the code structure.
sections.notes: AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) is Python's internal representation of code. Each node represents a syntax element like a function, class, or expression. The ast module provides tools to analyze and transform code programmatically.

sections.title: Learning Resources

items.description: Official Python AST documentation
Pydantic official docs for structured state

sections.code:
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional, List

class AgentState(BaseModel):
    agent_id: str
    current_task: Optional[str] = None
    completed_tasks: List[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    metadata: dict = Field(default_factory=dict)

# Usage
state = AgentState(agent_id="agent-001")
state.current_task = "Analyze repository"
print(state.model_dump())
sections.implementation: Create typed data models using Pydantic for agent state management. Define models for: 1) Agent configuration, 2) Task definitions, 3) Execution results. Use Field for validation and defaults.
sections.notes: Pydantic provides runtime validation and type coercion. It's essential for structured state in AI applications where you need guaranteed data shapes.

sections.title: Creative Learning

items.description: Draw AST tree diagrams showing how code maps to nodes
Parse real Python files and extract code metrics

sections.code:
import subprocess
import tempfile
import os

def run_git_command(repo_path: str, command: str) -> str:
    """Execute git command safely"""
    with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmpdir:
        result = subprocess.run(
            ['git', '-C', repo_path] + command.split(),
            capture_output=True,
            text=True,
            cwd=tmpdir
        )
        return result.stdout

# Usage
commits = run_git_command('/path/to/repo', 'log --oneline -10')
print(commits)
sections.implementation: Create a git automation script that: 1) Extracts commit history, 2) Analyzes commit patterns, 3) Generates a commit summary report. Use subprocess to call git commands safely.
sections.notes: Git automation enables forensic analysis of codebases. Understanding commit history helps trace the evolution of AI systems and identify patterns.

sections.title: Testing Ideas

items.description: Write tests for AST parser to verify class and function detection

sections.code:
import pytest
from ast_parser import ASTParser
import ast

def test_finds_classes():
    code = """class Agent:\n    def run(self):\n        pass"""
    tree = ast.parse(code)
    parser = ASTParser()
    parser.visit(tree)
    assert "Agent" in parser.classes

def test_finds_functions():
    code = """def initialize():\n    pass\n\ndef execute():\n    pass"""
    tree = ast.parse(code)
    parser = ASTParser()
    parser.visit(tree)
    assert "initialize" in parser.functions
    assert "execute" in parser.functions

def test_reports_missing_imports():
    code = """from nonexistent import Something"""
    # Implement import detection
    pass
sections.implementation: Write comprehensive tests for your AST parser: 1) Test class detection, 2) Test function detection, 3) Test import reporting. Use pytest fixtures for test code samples.
sections.notes: Testing is critical for AI tooling. Well-tested code ensures reliability in production AI systems.

modules.title: Git Forensic Tooling
modules.description: Extract and analyze commit history for AI systems

sections.title: What to learn
sections.code:
import subprocess
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class Commit:
    hash: str
    message: str
    author: str
    date: datetime
    files_changed: int

def get_commit_history(repo_path: str, limit: int = 100) -> List[Commit]:
    cmd = f'log --pretty=format:"%H|%s|%an|%ad|%h" --date=iso -n {limit}'
    result = subprocess.run(
        ['git', '-C', repo_path] + cmd.split('|'),
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    
    commits = []
    for line in result.stdout.strip().split('\n'):
        if line:
            hash_, message, author, date, files = line.split('|')
            commits.append(Commit(
                hash=hash_[:7],
                message=message,
                author=author,
                date=datetime.fromisoformat(date.strip()),
                files_changed=int(files)
            ))
    return commits
sections.implementation: Build a commit history extractor that: 1) Parses git log output, 2) Creates structured Commit objects, 3) Extracts metadata like author, date, message, and file changes.
sections.notes: Git forensics helps understand the development patterns of AI systems. Analyzing commit patterns reveals collaboration styles and code evolution.

sections.title: Creative Learning

items.description: Generate controlled git history and analyze it
Detect bulk upload vs incremental commits

sections.code:
def detect_commit_pattern(commits: List[dict]) -> str:
    """Detect if commits are bulk upload or incremental"""
    if not commits:
        return "unknown"
    
    # Calculate time gaps between commits
    time_gaps = []
    for i in range(1, len(commits)):
        gap = (commits[i]['date'] - commits[i-1]['date']).total_seconds()
        time_gaps.append(gap)
    
    avg_gap = sum(time_gaps) / len(time_gaps) if time_gaps else 0
    
    # Bulk upload: large files, short time between commits
    avg_files = sum(c['files_changed'] for c in commits) / len(commits)
    
    if avg_gap < 60 and avg_files > 10:  # Less than minute, many files
        return "bulk_upload"
    elif avg_gap > 3600:  # More than hour between commits
        return "incremental"
    else:
        return "standard"
sections.implementation: Build a pattern detector that: 1) Analyzes time gaps between commits, 2) Measures files changed per commit, 3) Classifies as bulk upload or incremental development.
sections.notes: Commit pattern analysis reveals development methodology. Bulk uploads often indicate initial project setup while incremental shows ongoing development.

sections.title: Testing Ideas

items.description: Test with single commit vs progressive commits repos

sections.code:
import pytest
from git_forensics import CommitAnalyzer, detect_commit_pattern

def test_single_commit_detection():
    commits = [
        {'date': '2024-01-01', 'files_changed': 50}
    ]
    pattern = detect_commit_pattern(commits)
    assert pattern == "bulk_upload"

def test_incremental_detection():
    from datetime import timedelta
    commits = [
        {'date': datetime(2024, 1, 1, 10, 0), 'files_changed': 2},
        {'date': datetime(2024, 1, 1, 12, 0), 'files_changed': 3},
        {'date': datetime(2024, 1, 1, 14, 0), 'files_changed': 1},
    ]
    pattern = detect_commit_pattern(commits)
    assert pattern == "incremental"
sections.implementation: Write tests for commit pattern detection using synthetic git histories. Test edge cases like single commits, rapid uploads, and spaced incremental commits.
sections.notes: Testing with synthetic data ensures your detector works correctly across different scenarios.

sections.title: What to learn
sections.code:
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from typing import TypedDict, List

class AgentState(TypedDict):
    messages: List[str]
    tasks: List[str]
    results: List[str]

def analyzer_node(state: AgentState) -> AgentState:
    """Analyze the input task"""
    return {
        **state,
        'tasks': ['analyze_code', 'extract_patterns']
    }

def parallel_worker_1(state: AgentState) -> AgentState:
    """Worker 1: Code analysis"""
    return {'results': state.get('results', []) + ['analysis_complete']}

def parallel_worker_2(state: AgentState) -> AgentState:
    """Worker 2: Pattern extraction"""
    return {'results': state.get('results', []) + ['patterns_extracted']}

def aggregator(state: AgentState) -> AgentState:
    """Combine results from parallel workers"""
    return {'messages': state.get('messages', []) + [f"Final: {state['results']}"]}

# Build the graph
graph = StateGraph(AgentState)
graph.add_node('analyzer', analyzer_node)
graph.add_node('worker_1', parallel_worker_1)
graph.add_node('worker_2', parallel_worker_2)
graph.add_node('aggregator', aggregator)

graph.set_entry_point('analyzer')
graph.add_edge('analyzer', 'worker_1')
graph.add_edge('analyzer', 'worker_2')
graph.add_edge('worker_1', 'aggregator')
graph.add_edge('worker_2', 'aggregator')
graph.add_edge('aggregator', END)

app = graph.compile()
sections.implementation: Build a LangGraph with: 1) Initial analyzer node, 2) Two parallel worker nodes (fan-out), 3) Aggregator node (fan-in), 4) Proper state management with reducers.
sections.notes: LangGraph enables complex multi-agent workflows. Understanding fan-out/fan-in patterns is essential for parallel AI processing.

sections.title: Learning Resources

items.description: Real Python LangGraph guide

sections.code:
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph
from typing import TypedDict
from operator import add

class CounterState(TypedDict):
    count: int

# WITHOUT reducer - overwrites!
def bad_increment(state: CounterState):
    return {'count': state['count'] + 1}

# WITH reducer - accumulates!
def good_increment(state: CounterState):
    return {'count': [state['count'][0] + 1]}  # Note: list for reducer

graph = StateGraph(CounterState)

# Add nodes - notice we pass the function directly
graph.add_node('increment', good_increment)
graph.set_entry_point('increment')
graph.add_edge('increment', 'increment')  # Loop!

app = graph.compile()

# State reducer ensures parallel nodes don't overwrite each other
state = app.invoke({'count': [0]}, {'recursion_limit': 5})
print(state)
sections.implementation: Create examples showing: 1) Why reducers are needed, 2) How to implement them, 3) Testing that parallel node outputs are combined correctly.
sections.notes: Reducers are critical in LangGraph. Without them, parallel nodes overwrite each other's state. With proper reducers, all outputs are combined.

sections.title: Testing Ideas

items.description: Test parallel node state mutations

sections.code:
import pytest
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph
from typing import TypedDict

class TestState(TypedDict):
    values: list

def worker_a(state):
    return {'values': ['a_result']}

def worker_b(state):
    return {'values': ['b_result']}

def combiner(state):
    # Should have both results!
    return {'values': state['values']}

# Build and test
graph = StateGraph(TestState)
graph.add_node('a', worker_a)
graph.add_node('b', worker_b)
graph.add_node('combine', combiner)
graph.set_entry_point('a')
graph.add_edge('a', 'b')
graph.add_edge('b', 'combine')

app = graph.compile()
result = app.invoke({'values': []})

# Verify both parallel results are present
assert 'a_result' in result['values'], "Worker A result missing!"
assert 'b_result' in result['values'], "Worker B result missing!"
sections.implementation: Write unit tests that: 1) Invoke graphs with test state, 2) Verify state mutations, 3) Check reducer behavior with parallel execution.
sections.notes: Testing graphs is essential. Always verify that state flows correctly through your nodes.

modules.title: PDF and Hallucination
modules.description: PDF parsing and hallucination detection in AI reports

sections.title: What to learn
sections.code:
import pdfplumber
from pathlib import Path

def extract_text_from_pdf(pdf_path: Path) -> str:
    """Extract all text from a PDF file"""
    text = ""
    with pdfplumber.open(pdf_path) as pdf:
        for page in pdf.pages:
            text += page.extract_text() + "\n"
    return text

def chunk_text(text: str, chunk_size: int = 500) -> list:
    """Split text into chunks for analysis"""
    words = text.split()
    chunks = []
    for i in range(0, len(words), chunk_size):
        chunks.append(' '.join(words[i:i+chunk_size]))
    return chunks

# Usage
pdf_text = extract_text_from_pdf(Path("report.pdf"))
chunks = chunk_text(pdf_text)
print(f"Extracted {len(chunks)} chunks")
sections.implementation: Build a PDF extractor that: 1) Uses pdfplumber to extract text, 2) Chunks text into manageable pieces, 3) Handles multi-page PDFs.
sections.notes: PDF extraction is foundational for analyzing AI-generated reports. Chunking enables granular analysis.

sections.title: Learning Resources

items.description: PyPDF2, pdfplumber tutorials

sections.code:
import pdfplumber
from PIL import Image
import io

def extract_images(pdf_path: str) -> list:
    """Extract all images from PDF"""
    images = []
    with pdfplumber.open(pdf_path) as pdf:
        for page_num, page in enumerate(pdf.pages):
            for img_index, img in enumerate(page.images):
                # Get image data
                x0, y0, x1, y1 = img['x0'], img['y0'], img['x1'], img['y1']
                bbox = (x0, y0, x1, y1)
                
                # Crop image from page
                crop = page.crop(bbox)
                img_data = crop.to_image(resolution=150)
                
                images.append({
                    'page': page_num + 1,
                    'index': img_index,
                    'data': img_data.original
                })
    
    return images

# Usage
images = extract_images("document.pdf")
print(f"Found {len(images)} images")
sections.implementation: Create an image extractor from PDFs: 1) Extract all images, 2) Track page numbers, 3) Save images for analysis.
sections.notes: Images may contain charts, diagrams, or visual evidence. Extracting them enables comprehensive analysis.

sections.title: Creative Learning

items.description: Load and chunk PDF text for rubric analysis

sections.code:
import pdfplumber
import re
from pathlib import Path

def find_file_references(text: str, repo_path: Path) -> dict:
    """Find file paths mentioned in text and verify they exist"""
    
    # Common patterns for file references
    patterns = [
        r'(?:file|path):\s*([/\w.-]+\.\w+)',
        r'`([/\w.-]+\.\w+)`',
        r'src/([/\w.-]+\.\w+)',
    ]
    
    found_files = []
    for pattern in patterns:
        matches = re.findall(pattern, text)
        found_files.extend(matches)
    
    # Verify each file exists
    results = {'valid': [], 'invalid': [], 'unverified': []}
    for file_path in set(found_files):
        full_path = repo_path / file_path
        if full_path.exists():
            results['valid'].append(file_path)
        elif not file_path.startswith('/') and not file_path.startswith('src'):
            results['unverified'].append(file_path)
        else:
            results['invalid'].append(file_path)
    
    return results

# Usage
text = "The agent uses src/agent.py and config/settings.json"
results = find_file_references(text, Path("./myrepo"))
sections.implementation: Build a cross-reference tool that: 1) Finds file path references in text, 2) Checks if files exist in the repository, 3) Reports invalid references.
sections.notes: Cross-referencing detects hallucinations where AI claims files exist that don't.

sections.title: Testing Ideas

items.description: Test hallucination detection with fake vs real references

sections.code:
import pytest
from hallucination_detector import find_file_references
from pathlib import Path
from unittest.mock import MagicMock

def test_detects_nonexistent_file():
    """Should flag references to files that don't exist"""
    text = "The system uses src/nonexistent.py"
    repo = MagicMock(spec=Path)
    repo.__truediv__ = lambda self, x: MagicMock(exists=lambda: False)
    
    results = find_file_references(text, repo)
    assert 'src/nonexistent.py' in results['invalid']

def test_accepts_valid_file():
    """Should accept references to existing files"""
    text = "The agent uses src/main.py"
    
    def mock_exists():
        return True
    
    mock_path = MagicMock()
    mock_path.exists = mock_exists
    repo = MagicMock()
    repo.__truediv__ = lambda self, x: mock_path
    
    results = find_file_references(text, repo)
    assert 'src/main.py' in results['valid']
sections.implementation: Write tests for hallucination detection: 1) Test with fake file references, 2) Test with valid references, 3) Test edge cases like partial matches.
sections.notes: Testing ensures your detector catches hallucinations reliably.

modules.title: Structured LLM Output
modules.description: JSON schemas and prompt engineering for AI outputs

sections.title: What to learn
sections.implementation: Build a structured output system with: 1) Pydantic model for evaluation results, 2) Prompt template for structured judgments, 3) Response parser with retry logic.
sections.notes: Structured output is essential for AI systems. Pydantic ensures type safety and validation.

sections.title: Learning Resources

items.description: Structured output documentation

sections.code:
from langchain.output_parsers import StructuredOutputParser, ResponseSchema

# Define expected output schema
response_schemas = [
    ResponseSchema(name="score", description="Evaluation score from 1-5"),
    ResponseSchema(name="argument", description="Reasoning for the evaluation"),
    ResponseSchema(name="cited_evidence", description="List of evidence IDs cited"),
]

parser = StructuredOutputParser(response_schemas=response_schemas)

# Create prompt with format instructions
format_instructions = parser.get_format_instructions()

prompt = f"""
Evaluate the following code quality.

Code: {code_snippet}

{format_instructions}
"""

# Use with LLM
# response = llm.invoke(prompt)
# result = parser.parse(response)
sections.implementation: Implement structured output using LangChain: 1) Define ResponseSchema, 2) Create output parser, 3) Inject format instructions into prompts.
sections.notes: LangChain's output parsers simplify structured response handling.

sections.title: Testing Ideas

items.description: Test retry on malformed JSON

sections.code:
import pytest
from structured_output import parse_llm_response, EvaluationResult

def test_valid_json_parses():
    response = '''
    {
        "score": 4,
        "argument": "Good code structure",
        "cited_evidence": ["src/main.py"]
    }
    '''
    result = parse_llm_response(response)
    assert result.score == 4
    assert result.argument == "Good code structure"

def test_malformed_json_triggers_retry():
    # First two attempts are invalid
    responses = [
        "Here's my evaluation: { score: 3 }",  # Invalid
        "score=3, argument=bad",  # Invalid
        '{"score": 3, "argument": "good", "cited_evidence": []}',  # Valid
    ]
    
    # Would need mock to cycle through responses
    result = parse_llm_response(responses[0], retries=3)
    # Should eventually succeed or return None
    assert result is None or isinstance(result, EvaluationResult)
sections.implementation: Write tests for: 1) Valid JSON parsing, 2) Malformed JSON handling, 3) Retry behavior, 4) Edge cases like missing fields.
sections.notes: Testing output parsing is critical. Malformed responses happen frequently with LLMs.

sections.title: What to learn

sections.notes: The synthesis engine is the 'Chief Justice' - it resolves conflicts and enforces deterministic rules.

items.description: LLM + deterministic rules articles

sections.title: Creative Learning

items.description: Generate sample opinions and test verdict logic

sections.code:
# Generate test opinions
def generate_test_opinions() -> List[JudgeOpinion]:
    return [
        JudgeOpinion(
            judge_id="prosecutor",
            score=1,
            argument="Security flaw in authentication module",
            evidence=["auth.py:45"]
        ),
        JudgeOpinion(
            judge_id="defense",
            score=5,
            argument="Deep metacognition achieved",
            evidence=[]  # No evidence!
        ),
        JudgeOpinion(
            judge_id="tech_lead",
            score=5,
            argument="Excellent architecture, scalable design",
            evidence=["architecture.md", "design.png"]
        ),
    ]

# Test the engine
engine = SynthesisEngine()
engine.add_rule(security_override)
engine.add_rule(dissent_rule)  # Must have 2+ judges agree

opinions = generate_test_opinions()
result = engine.synthesize(opinions)
print(result)
# Should: Security override triggers -> REJECTED
sections.implementation: Create synthetic test cases: 1) Generate various judge opinion combinations, 2) Test each rule triggers correctly, 3) Verify final verdicts.
sections.notes: Synthetic testing reveals edge cases in your synthesis logic.

sections.title: Testing Ideas

items.description: Test rule overrides with sample opinions

sections.code:
import pytest
from synthesis_engine import SynthesisEngine, JudgeOpinion, Verdict

def test_security_override_triggers():
    """Security issues should override other opinions"""
    opinions = [
        JudgeOpinion("p", 5, "Great code", []),
        JudgeOpinion("d", 4, "Minor issues", []),
        JudgeOpinion("t", 1, "SQL injection vulnerability", []),
    ]
    
    engine = SynthesisEngine()
    engine.add_rule(security_override)
    
    result = engine.synthesize(opinions)
    assert result['verdict'] == Verdict.REJECTED

def test_dissent_requires_majority():
    """Dissent needs at least 2 judges to agree"""
    opinions = [
        JudgeOpinion("p", 1, "bad", []),
        JudgeOpinion("d", 5, "good", []),
        JudgeOpinion("t", 5, "good", []),
    ]
    
    result = engine.synthesize(opinions)
    assert result['verdict'] == Verdict.APPROVED  # 2 vs 1

def test_no_evidence_overruled():
    """Claims without evidence should be overruled"""
    opinions = [
        JudgeOpinion("p", 5, "Deep metacognition", []),  # No evidence!
        JudgeOpinion("d", 5, "Great", []),
    ]
    
    result = engine.synthesize(opinions)
    assert result['final_score'] < 5  # Should be capped
sections.implementation: Write tests for synthesis rules: 1) Security override, 2) Dissent handling, 3) Evidence requirements, 4) Score capping.
sections.notes: Comprehensive testing ensures your synthesis engine behaves correctly in all scenarios.
